lesson.menu_desc: The causative: have/get something done -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 20 -- Getting Things Sorted Abroad
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 20 -- Getting Things Sorted Abroad

extra_audio.text: First, Pricila settled into her new apartment, but there was a lot to get sorted. Then she had the locks changed and got the internet set up, after a little red tape with the paperwork. Next, she booked an appointment at a salon and finally had her hair cut the way she likes it. After that, she dropped her winter coat off at the dry cleaner's and had it cleaned. So now, with everything sorted, she feels completely at home abroad.

listenings.text: When you first move somewhere new, the to-do list is endless. My first week abroad, I felt like I spent the whole time getting things sorted. The apartment was lovely, but nothing was quite ready. On the first morning I had the locks changed, because the landlord recommended it, and I got the internet set up so I could finally video-call my family. There was a bit of red tape, of course. I had to have a few documents translated and certified, and the paperwork took longer than I expected. By the middle of the week I was getting the hang of it. I booked an appointment at a little salon around the corner and had my hair cut, which made me feel human again. I took my old winter coat to the dry cleaner's, dropped it off, and had it cleaned. I even got the car checked before a long drive, and the mechanic gave me a fair quote. The strange thing is, none of this is glamorous, but it's exactly what makes a place feel like home. You don't really live somewhere until you've had things fixed, set up, and sorted in your own name. After twenty lessons, I can honestly arrange all of it in English now, and that feels like the whole journey coming together.

Hi Pricila, it's me, just leaving you a quick voice note now that you've moved in. You asked me where to get things done around the neighbourhood, so here are my honest recommendations. For your hair, there's a lovely salon on the main street. Book an appointment a few days ahead, because the good stylist gets busy. If you need documents translated for the paperwork, don't use the big agency near the station. They charge a fortune. There's a small office two streets away that does it properly and gives you a written quote first. For the car, take it to Tony's garage. He'll get it fixed honestly and won't invent problems. And if you want the apartment painted before winter, my neighbour did his last year. He had the whole place done in two days and it cost less than you'd think. Oh, and one tip. When you sign up for the internet, ask them to set everything up for you so you don't have to wrestle with it yourself. There's always a bit of red tape, but once it's all sorted, you'll feel completely settled in. Welcome to the neighbourhood. Call me if you need anything.

paras: There is a moment, somewhere in the first weeks abroad, when the excitement of arriving gives way to a long and unglamorous list. The flights are booked, the photos are taken -- and now the taps need fixing, the documents need translating, and the internet stubbornly refuses to work. This is the quiet, real business of settling in. Nobody writes postcards about having the locks changed or getting a SIM card set up, yet this is exactly the work that turns a strange address into a home. You stop being a tourist the day you first have something fixed in your own name.

The language of all this is wonderfully practical. We rarely do these jobs ourselves; we arrange for someone else to do them. So we say we are having the apartment painted, getting the car serviced, or having a few papers translated. The grammar is small but it changes everything: instead of "I painted the wall", which means you held the brush, we say "I had the wall painted", which means a professional did it for you. Once you can hear that difference, a whole world of errands, quotes, deposits and appointments opens up to you in English -- the world of actually living somewhere, not just visiting it.

And there is, of course, the red tape. Every country has its forms, its stamps, its offices that close for lunch exactly when you arrive. The paperwork can be maddening, and the temptation is to feel defeated by it. But there is a strange satisfaction in getting it all sorted -- in dropping the coat off, picking up the quote, signing up for the service, and finally crossing the last item off the list. The reward is not the clean apartment or the working internet. The reward is the feeling, on some ordinary evening, of looking around a place that now works, in a language you now speak, and realising that you have made yourself at home in the world.

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main point about settling in abroad?
choices: Travelling is only worthwhile if you avoid all the boring errands and paperwork once you arrive somewhere new.
The unglamorous work of getting things sorted -- having things fixed, translated and set up -- is exactly what turns a strange place into a home.
It is always better to do every job yourself rather than arrange for a professional to do it for you.

items: The writer says you stop being a tourist the day you first have something fixed in your own name.
t
You stop being a tourist the day you first have something fixed in your own name.
According to the writer, "I had the wall painted" means that you painted the wall yourself.
f
"I had the wall painted" means a professional did it for you, not that you held the brush yourself.
The writer says nobody writes postcards about having the locks changed or getting a SIM card set up.
t
Nobody writes postcards about having the locks changed or getting a SIM card set up, yet this is the work that turns a strange address into a home.
The writer believes the real reward of settling in is simply having a clean apartment and working internet.
f
The reward is not the clean apartment or the working internet; it is the feeling of having made yourself at home in the world.

matching.title: Match each expression to its meaning
defs: To get used to a new home or place and feel comfortable
To deal with something successfully so it is no longer a problem
A small task you do outside the home, like going to the bank
A company or person you pay to do a job, like a plumber
A price someone gives you in advance for a piece of work
Official rules and forms that make something slow and annoying
To install or arrange something so it is ready to use
The official forms and documents you have to complete

rephrase.title: Rewrite using the causative (have / get something done)
items: A hairdresser cut my hair yesterday. -> I ... (have) yesterday.
I had my hair cut yesterday.
A professional is going to translate my documents. -> I'm going to ... (get).
I'm going to get my documents translated.
Someone is painting our apartment this week. -> We ... (have) this week.
We're having our apartment painted this week.
A mechanic needs to fix the car. -> I need to ... (get).
I need to get the car fixed.
Someone stole her bag at the station. (experience) -> She ... (have).
She had her bag stolen at the station.
A technician will set up the internet tomorrow. -> I'm going to ... (have).
I'm going to have the internet set up tomorrow.

answerkey.title: Reveal the causative answer key
list: I had my hair cut yesterday.
I'm going to get my documents translated.
We're having our apartment painted this week.
I need to get the car fixed.
She had her bag stolen at the station.
I'm going to have the internet set up tomorrow.
answerkey.note: The causative = have / get + OBJECT + past participle. We use it when we ARRANGE for someone else to do a job, instead of doing it ourselves. Compare: "I cut my hair" (I did it) vs "I had my hair cut" (the hairdresser did it for me). Form in any tense: have/get changes for the tense (had / am having / will have / am going to get), but the second verb is ALWAYS the past participle (cut, translated, painted, fixed, set up). 'Have' and 'get' mean the same here, but 'get' is a little more informal. There is also an EXPERIENCE causative for bad things that happen TO you: "She had her bag stolen", "He had his flight cancelled" -- here you did not arrange it; it happened to you. Do not confuse this with the plain passive ("The car was fixed"): the causative keeps YOU as the person who arranged the service.

analyse.title: Close reading
items: The writer says "You stop being a tourist the day you first have something fixed in your own name." What does this mean, and do you agree that errands and paperwork are part of really living somewhere?

The text explains the difference between "I painted the wall" and "I had the wall painted". Why is this difference useful when you live abroad? Give one example from your own life using the causative.

After twenty lessons, the writer says she can "arrange all of it in English now". Looking back on this whole journey, what is one real-life thing abroad you now feel confident you could get sorted in English?
